title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply rooted in the local pūrākau (legend) of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe. According to Ngāti Kahungunu tradition, the stream was named after the native pigeon (kererū) that would gather to drink from its waters. Students will explore how this pūrākau informs our kaitiaki responsibilities today, particularly addressing the modern challenge of sediment runoff from local farms affecting the stream's mauri (life force).
learningObjectives: Identify and use at least 10 Māori environmental terms (e.g., mauri, kaitiaki, awa, taonga) in context when discussing the local stream
Create a simple pepeha that includes their personal connection to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Collaboratively develop a basic Māori environmental assessment using the 'Mauri Model' framework for the local stream
Present findings and recommendations in te reo Māori using appropriate sentence structures for environmental reporting

activities.title: Whakatau me te Pepeha - Welcome and Connection
activities.description: Students begin with a karakia to the awa, then learn and share a modified pepeha that includes their relationship with Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Using sentence structures like 'Ko [name] ahau, ko Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku tōku awa' to establish personal connection.

activities.title: Pūrākau o Te Awa - Story of the Stream
activities.description: Local kaumātua shares the pūrākau of how the stream got its name and the traditional practices of monitoring its health. Students learn specific vocabulary (kōtukutuku, kererū, mauri, wai māori) and practice using these terms in sentences describing the stream's history.

activities.title: Tiaki Taiao - Environmental Monitoring
activities.description: Field trip to the stream where students use Māori environmental indicators (clarity of water, presence of native species, water temperature) alongside basic scientific measures. They record observations in both English and te reo Māori using prepared templates with sentence starters.

activities.title: Hei Kaitiaki - Becoming Guardians
activities.description: Working in small groups, students develop simple recommendations for protecting the awa using the Mauri Model framework (considering mauri of water, ecosystem, and community). They create posters with te reo Māori captions and present their kaitiaki plan to the class.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua Hone Smith as cultural advisor and pūrākau sharer
Te Awa Tupua - Whanganui River Claims Settlement Act 2017 as legal framework example
NIWA's 'Mauri Model' environmental assessment framework documents
Local farm representative to discuss practical conservation measures

tasks: Create a visual presentation (poster or digital) showing the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using both Māori and scientific indicators
Deliver a 2-minute oral presentation in te reo Māori explaining one recommendation for protecting the awa
Complete a written reflection connecting the pūrākau to modern kaitiaki responsibilities using at least 8 new vocabulary words correctly

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Level 4: Use basic Māori patterns and structures to communicate about immediate and familiar environment
Social Sciences: Place and Environment - Level 4: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
Science: Living World - Level 4: Explore how scientific knowledge and Māori knowledge systems inform each other
Key Competencies: Participating and Contributing - Students will contribute to the care of their local environment
